title: Hazel pea sticks
subtitle: Coppiced hazel support sticks for climbing peas and beans
excerpt: Hazel pea sticks are the traditional kitchen-garden support for climbing peas and dwarf beans: branched hazel rods pushed into the soil in rows, providing a natural trellis that composted at the end of the season. This project covers cutting and preparing a full-season supply from a small coppice bundle.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for kitchen garden support methods; Victorian kitchen garden manuals for pea stick traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: Woodland management technique in which trees are cut to the ground on a rotation; hazel is cut every 7 to 12 years, producing a crop of straight rods ideal for garden and craft use.

content.text: Hazel pea sticks are one of the most direct connections between

craft and the kitchen garden. A naturally branched hazel rod provides exactly the structure a climbing pea needs: a central stem to wrap around, and side branches that catch and hold the tendrils without tying. At the end of the season, the sticks are pulled up and composted; there is nothing to retrieve, store, or wash.

Cut hazel rods in late winter or early spring before the sap rises and the catkins open; this is the traditional coppice season. Select second-year or older rods with natural side branches every 10 to 15 cm. The ideal pea stick is 120 to 150 cm long, with branches covering the full length. Thicker rods (2 cm base diameter or more) make bean poles rather than pea sticks; choose rods 1 to 1.5 cm in diameter at the base.
Trim the side branches at the base: remove the lowest 30 cm of branches entirely. This bare section goes into the soil; a buried branch rots faster and can cause the stick to heave during frosts.
Cut the base at a sharp angle with secateurs: the angled cut makes pushing the stick into the ground easier. A flat cut will resist the soil.
Trim the tip if it extends more than 20 cm above the pea variety's expected height. The peas should not grow taller than the sticks.

Push sticks into the soil in a double row, 20 to 25 cm apart, alternating sides so the branches of each stick interlock with the next. The interlocked branches form a stable trellis that does not need cross-ties or string. For climbing peas in a single row, use one stick per 2 pea plants.

Hazel pea sticks can be stored dry for one further season if the rods are still firm when pulled up. Trim off any rotted section at the base and re-cut the angle. Sticks that have become brittle or show significant rot at the base go to the compost.
Bean poles: longer, thicker rods (2 m, 2 cm diameter) from the same coppice cut; used in a wigwam for runner beans.
Sweet pea frame: sticks arranged in a circle for a decorative sweet pea tower; all tips tied at the top with a length of willow withy.

items.symptom: Sticks blow over in wind before the plants have grabbed them
items.cause: Sticks not pushed deep enough into the soil; or the soil is too light
items.fix: Push sticks 35 to 40 cm into the soil; this requires a firm push and sometimes a mallet on hard-compacted ground. On very light sandy soil, tie the top 5 cm of alternate pairs of sticks together with a willow withy to form a continuous low cordon.
